content:
5 min read
Just now

--

There was a brief golden age, roughly two years long, when the smartest software on Earth cost the same as a large pizza. Twenty dollars a month bought what felt like unlimited intelligence. We pasted in fifty pages, asked twenty follow-up questions, and never once thought about what any of it cost to run. It was the closest thing modern technology has offered to a free lunch, and we treated it accordingly.
That age is ending, not with an announcement but with a meter. One afternoon, a long chat stops mid-thought, and a timer appears. Or a small balance called “usage credits” starts draining while we work. The subscription we bought as a product has quietly turned into a utility bill.
And somewhere between the pizza and the utility bill, the economics of AI stopped hiding from us.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image: Author/Google StudioThe Cheapest Tokens in History, and the Biggest Bills
Here is the strange part: the raw price of AI has been collapsing. Guido Appenzeller at Andreessen Horowitz called the trend LLMflation, and estimated that a model of constant quality gets about 10 times cheaper every year. By that math, intelligence should feel nearly free by now.
Our bills disagree, and the reason is volume. We are no longer sending one question and…
